summary:
Prostate cancer primarily develops in the peripheral zone; rarely does it occur in the central zone as per historical descriptions from 1968.
 Agent2's reasoning (Round 2): The information provided indicates a strong correlation between tumor location within different zones of the prostate gland and prognosis for patients diagnosed with this disease type. Specifically, article references highlight significant differences based upon where tumors are located—namely higher incidences observed more frequently outside versus inside certain regions such as those mentioned earlier related specifically towards peripheral vs central areas respectively...
 
 Summary: Prognosis varies significantly depending on whether prostate cancer originates in the peripheral or central region since most cases arise in the former while being rare in latter according to established medical literature dating back several decades now.
 To summarize concisely:

Prostate cancer typically occurs in the peripheral zone rather than the less common occurrence seen centrally, aligning closely across multiple sources reviewed here. This distribution impacts patient outcomes substantially when considering treatment options available currently. 

The summary has been refined into two clear statements focusing directly on critical aspects pertinent to understanding which statement accurately reflects current knowledge concerning primary sites of prostate carcinogenesis relative to overall survival rates post-diagnosis among affected individuals undergoing standard care protocols globally practiced nowadays too!
